editorialSummary.text: Dim sum, traditional Chinese dishes & chef specialties in a spacious setting with white tablecloths.

text.text: Delicious dim sum served in a comfortable, large dining room. We were a party of 5 and arrived at 11am on Sunday morning. It was crowded but we only had to wait 5 minutes for our table. The quality of the dim sum was good. Popular items like har gao and shiumai were larger than we are accustomed to from Hong Kong and New York Chinatown. Yet they were tasty. The service was a bit slow as waiters seemed over stretched with the full house. They were nevertheless friendly and courteous.

originalText.text: Delicious dim sum served in a comfortable, large dining room. We were a party of 5 and arrived at 11am on Sunday morning. It was crowded but we only had to wait 5 minutes for our table. The quality of the dim sum was good. Popular items like har gao and shiumai were larger than we are accustomed to from Hong Kong and New York Chinatown. Yet they were tasty. The service was a bit slow as waiters seemed over stretched with the full house. They were nevertheless friendly and courteous.

text.text:
I recently tried Lunasia Dim Sum House for the first time. I was curious to visit because I noticed that the restaurant has been selected by the Michelin Guide for two different years, and it has also been featured in newspapers as one of the places known for its dim sum.

When I looked at the menu, the prices were a bit on the higher side, so I was expecting something really standout or memorable.

The food overall was good. The pork siomai tasted good and well-made, but for me it was nothing particularly special that would make me specifically come back just for that dish.
The chicken feet were very soft, flavorful, and nicely prepared. The seaweed salad was also good, and the roasted duck had a pleasant flavor.

Everything we tried was good and enjoyable, but personally I was looking for that one dish that would really stand out and make me say, “Let’s go back to Lunasia because it’s amazing.”
I didn’t quite find that moment, although the food quality was solid overall.

That said, if you are in Alhambra, this is still a nice place to try, especially if you want to experience a restaurant that has been recognized by the Michelin Guide and is known locally for dim sum.

Overall: good food, good experience — just not something that felt particularly unique for the price and The service was decent, but it would be nice if the staff were a bit more friendly and attentive to customers’ needs. There is still some room for improvement.

text.text:
Enjoyed the portions for the dim sum as they were quite large (jumbo sized) and the meats were juicy, especially enjoyed the siu mai. The egg tofu with abolone sauce and shrimp and scallop were also my favorites. I would also recommend getting their entrees, which are also delicious. The string beans were also a great texture and still crunchy while being well seasoned. We also tried the pork garlic spare rib and had a great crisp outside with flavorful chewy meat on the inside.

Went for dinner on a Saturday night and didn't even have to wait for a table!

text.text: Lunasia has an excellent reputation for dim sum style real Chinese food (not Westernized) and we were not disappointed.  The menu is extensive with many seafood and other dishes in addition to dim sum.  Each dish was impeccably prepared, and served quickly (it was not busy). We liked each dish.  Even if you're not into dim sum, they have traditional dishes such as sweet and sour chicken and beef chow fun which we enjoyed.  I especially loved the fried soft shell crab.  We had excellent service -  I dropped my chopsticks and no kidding, before I was able to retrieve them a server was already giving me a new set.  This would also be a great place for special occasions because they offer over the top dishes such as whole suckling pig.  My only issue is that they ask you to order from an online menu and that took a lot of time as it was our first time (I think they will provide paper menus on request).

text.text:
Decided to spend a valentine day here, due to the fact was craving Cantonese food. Place is great, spacious. Looking at their wall seeing a frame from Los Angeles time which in my head this must be a real deal.

Coming in can hear the servers speaking in hoisan/guangdong language which brought a sense of home as a Cantonese speaker.

My girlfriend and I placed our reservation for 4:20 which they weren’t busy pretty open seating was placed in a corner wasn’t appreciated it of it but it’s okay. And this was also her first time experiencing an experience like this which I’m glad to of came to a place to actually try it together as I’m very particular of where I’m getting dimsum from.

What I had her tried was
Hot and sour soup - 10/10 very good, it’s hard to find a place to messed up this recipe.
Chicken feet - 9/10  this is really good but texture was bit throwing me off but the price you pay is really worth the quantity.
Beef tripe - 8.5/10 the sauce felt abit flavorless but was so much tripe tho so it was a plus there.
Sui mai shrimp& pork - 9/10 good no complaints
Chow fun beef - amazing 10/10
Sticky rice - 9/10 no complaints
Steam bun 10/10 good buns
Rice noodle with bbq - 9/10

Everything that we tried was spectacular. The only con is I wish they kinda explain the ordering instead of dropping a check book with the QR code on there. When ordering I thought I was placing a to go order instead. As well for the check i wish they charged based on who drinking cause i was charged 2 tea when I was the only person drinking it. Chili oil is complimentary you do have to pay abit for it.

But overall the experience was great, I enjoyed being my gf first  yum cha experience and she enjoyed all her favorite one was the steam bun.
Service can be here and there I was check on by different server than the one assigned.
